text: Here you can clearly identify the abdominal wall metastasis at the medial portion of the patient's previous colostomy closure site.
There is no evidence of peritoneal metastasis.
We begin by scoring the mass with monopolar scissors and begin excising it carefully in a circumferential manner.
By applying traction and counter traction, we are able to stay in the correct plane and continue our dissection of the mass.
By using the bipolar grasper to elevate the abdomen, we're able to dissect the anterior surface of the mass, ensuring we stay in the correct plane.
Here we begin the lateral dissection of the mass using the same principles of traction and counter traction.
Our dissection is meticulous and we assure excellent hemostasis.
The mass is removed in its entirety.
A small skin incision is made at the site of the previous colostomy closure and a ring forcept is then introduced into the abdomen used to grasp the mass and extract it.
This is then passed off the table as a specimen.
However, we do identify a remnant of the abdominal wall mass in our resection cavity.
We elect excises in a similar fashion to ensure complete resection of the abdominal wall metastasis.
The defect in the abdominal wall is closed with a 2-0 barbed suture.
Taking evenly spaced bites on either side, the closure is done in a continuous running fashion.
Once we get to the end of the defect, the suture is then tightened and the needle is now cut off.
A second 2-0 barbed suture is used to complete our two-layered closure.
This is also done in a running continuous fashion.
This achieves an airtight closure with a good approximation of the fascial layers.
At the end of the closure, both needles are extracted via our skin incision site.
The skin incisions were closed with a running monofilament suture and this concluded the case.
